agency_name: Department of Employment and Workplace Relations (DEWR)
executive_summary: After three years leading major reforms across employment, skills and workplace relations, DEWR's stated focus for 2025-26 is delivery — making existing initiatives produce measurable outcomes. The department is a hybrid policy/service-delivery entity (~4,454 APS staff) running employment services to ~700,000 jobseekers, VET and apprenticeship programs, workplace-relations policy, and a newly assumed role administering the CFMEU's Construction and General Division. Strong BD signals sit in: embedding employment services reform and fixing the mutual-obligations compliance system (acutely Robodebt-sensitive), an Integrity Roadmap (2025-27), a governance-framework review, expanding AI/data deployment, ICT modernisation and Essential Eight cyber uplift, and maturing a performance/regulatory framework. The major constraint is the Strategic Commissioning Framework: DEWR is deliberately reducing outsourcing of core work (policy, legal, finance, service delivery), so credible opportunities skew toward independent assurance, ICT/digital build, evaluation and specialist surge rather than core advisory.

strategic_priorities.title: Shift from reform design to delivery
strategic_priorities.description: DEWR explicitly pivots from three years of reform to ensuring those reforms deliver effective outcomes — implementing election commitments and embedding employment services reforms (parents, paid work placements, justice-to-employment transitions).
strategic_priorities.source_quote: This year... DEWR will focus on delivery. Over the past 3 years, DEWR has led significant reforms... Our priority now is to ensure these initiatives deliver effective, meaningful outcomes.

strategic_priorities.title: Fix the mutual obligations compliance system
strategic_priorities.description: Ensuring the mutual obligations compliance system 'operates as intended' with a fair, transparent framework — a politically charged area given the Robodebt legacy.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: This includes critical work to ensure the mutual obligations compliance system operates as intended. Our goal is a fair, transparent framework.

strategic_priorities.title: Joined-up tertiary system and VET reform
strategic_priorities.description: Working with the new Australian Tertiary Education Commission, JSA and Department of Education on a tertiary system roadmap aligning VET and higher education to labour-market needs; delivering Free TAFE and apprenticeship incentive reform.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: Explore opportunities for a more joined up tertiary system where our VET and higher education sectors are better aligned.

strategic_priorities.title: AI adoption — internal and national policy
strategic_priorities.description: Expanding internal AI deployment to lift productivity and service delivery, while contributing to national AI workforce policy via the tripartite AI Working Group.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: the department intends to expand the deployment of AI technologies to augment and enhance our work and drive productivity.

strategic_priorities.title: Construction industry / CFMEU administration
strategic_priorities.description: Driving structural and cultural reform in construction through the National Construction Industry Forum and administering the CFMEU Construction and General Division and its branches.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: we will continue to drive structural and cultural reform through the National Construction Industry Forum and the scheme for the administration of the... CFMEU.

strategic_priorities.title: Integrity maturity uplift
strategic_priorities.description: Implementing the Integrity Roadmap (2025-2027) developed with an independent advisor, including Robodebt Royal Commission recommendations and conflict-of-interest/PID capabilities.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: The Integrity Roadmap... outlines a 3-year pathway (2025–2027) to strengthen integrity maturity across the department.

strategic_priorities.title: New and evolving regulatory functions
strategic_priorities.description: Progressing a national labour hire licensing scheme, maturing the department's regulatory performance framework, and reviewing multiple legislative frameworks (Secure Jobs Better Pay, Closing Loopholes, SRC Act, JSA enabling legislation).
strategic_priorities.source_quote: Progress a national labour hire licensing scheme to protect workers and reduce regulatory complexity.

reform_and_change_drivers: Government economic-growth agenda focused on lifting productivity, building resilience and budget sustainability
Persistent structural labour-market challenges — one-third of occupations in national shortage, 61.4% of employment-services participants in the system over 12 months
Net zero transition requiring workforce reskilling and place-based community support
Multiple legislative reviews: Secure Jobs Better Pay Act, Closing Loopholes Act, SRC Act 1988 (Comcare), and JSA enabling legislation
Robodebt Royal Commission response and broader public-sector integrity reform agenda
APS Reform agenda and the Strategic Commissioning Framework reducing outsourcing of core work
National Skills Agreement shared-stewardship model and creation of the Australian Tertiary Education Commission
Closing the Gap — new First Nations Executive Board governance and 2025 Implementation Plan commitments
Accelerating AI adoption creating both internal productivity demand and national policy/regulatory pressure
challenges_and_risks: Delivery risk: ability to deliver programs on time/budget amid reforms, competing priorities and 'reduced funding' / resourcing constraints
Mutual obligations compliance system must be demonstrably fair and transparent — high public and political scrutiny post-Robodebt
People risk: competitive labour market, difficulty attracting/retaining skilled staff, and staff burnout / psychosocial safety
ICT risk: inability to meet demand for skilled ICT labour, ageing/historical systems, cyber and concentration threats, funding pressure on ICT program
Information and data risk: breaches, unauthorised access, data bias including from AI use
Finance and regulatory risk: budget constraints, fraud/corruption/privacy exposure, and legislative compliance capability gaps
Strategic Commissioning Framework limits use of external providers for core policy, legal, finance and service-delivery work
Stakeholder fatigue across an unusually broad partnership network (unions, employers, states, First Nations, international)

opportunity_areas.area: Independent assurance of the mutual obligations compliance system
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Ensure the compliance system 'operates as intended' and is demonstrably fair and transparent in the shadow of Robodebt
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Independent, gateway-style assurance / health check of the mutual obligations compliance system design, decision logic and controls — confirming fairness, transparency and legal-framework alignment, with a findings-and-recommendations report
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan names this as 'critical work' and the Integrity Roadmap explicitly implements Robodebt Royal Commission recommendations. Independent assurance is exactly the kind of demand a high-scrutiny, automation-adjacent compliance program generates, and assurance sits outside the Strategic Commissioning Framework's core-work restrictions.

opportunity_areas.area: Delivery support for employment services reform and place-based trials
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Embed reforms and run trials (Broome, Thrive Hubs, Dandenong) producing evidence for broader reform
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Delivery assurance / benefits-management framework and PMO support for the employment services reform portfolio and place-based trials, with stage-gate reviews feeding the evidence base
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan lists multiple named trials and a commitment to 'continuous enhancement of service delivery' under explicit delivery-risk and resourcing pressure, creating time-bound program-delivery demand.

opportunity_areas.area: Evaluation of place-based and employment services trials
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Generate 'ongoing evidence which can be considered in broader employment services reforms'
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A process and outcome evaluation of the Thrive Hubs, Dandenong Employment Hub and Broome trial — evaluation plan plus report to inform scaling decisions
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan explicitly frames trials as evidence-generators for reform. Evaluation is a discrete specialist engagement that complements (rather than substitutes for) in-house policy work under the SCF.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Review whether the 2024 governance framework and structure is 'operating as intended'
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: An independent governance / committee-effectiveness assessment of the 2024 governance framework and structure, with recommendations to refine roles, decision rights and committee operation
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan states the department 'will review the implementation of the governance framework and structure' in 2025-26 — a named, time-bound review well-suited to a short independent advisory.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Deliver the 3-year Integrity Roadmap (2025-2027), including COI/PID capabilities and Robodebt recommendations
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Independent assurance / maturity health check of Integrity Roadmap implementation milestones, including conflict-of-interest and public interest disclosure capability uplift
opportunity_areas.rationale: The roadmap is explicitly multi-year and was developed 'in partnership with an independent integrity advisor', signalling appetite for external assurance support across the period.

opportunity_areas.area: AI deployment — build and data analytics
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Expand AI to improve operations, service delivery and data insights, safely and ethically
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Discovery and service design plus solution build/integration for AI-augmented workflows, paired with Data, Analytics & Information support for advanced analytics and AI governance guardrails
opportunity_areas.rationale: The Secretary commits to 'expand the deployment of AI technologies' and the data section commits to leveraging AI for policy/program/evaluation. The firm's ability to build (not just advise) fits, and DEWR already relies heavily on ICT labour hire (653 of its external workforce).

opportunity_areas.area: ICT modernisation and cyber Essential Eight uplift
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Modernise legacy/on-prem and cloud infrastructure; raise Essential Eight maturity; manage cyber and concentration threats
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Essential Eight security assessment and assurance of systems and data, plus advisory on cyber maturity uplift across on-prem and public-cloud environments
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan names a focus on 'maturity levels against the Australian Cyber Security Centre's Essential Eight' and identifies ICT security as Strategic Risk 7. DEWR also runs a PROTECTED shared-services environment for other agencies, raising the stakes.

opportunity_areas.area: Data strategy delivery and analytics capability
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Deliver 'greater value from our data' via integration, advanced analytics and AI under the Data Strategy 2024-27
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Data analytics and business-intelligence delivery, plus information-management and data-governance support aligned to the Chief Data Officer's 2025-26 priorities (sharing, integration, evaluation capacity)
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan sets a clear second-year ambition for the Data Strategy and names the CDO and Data Governance Committee as owners — a defined, specialist data demand.

opportunity_areas.area: Regulatory performance framework and labour hire licensing scheme
opportunity_areas.agency_need: Mature a fit-for-purpose regulatory performance framework and stand up a national labour hire licensing scheme
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Design support for a PGPA-aligned regulatory performance framework and operating-model/measures design for the new labour hire licensing function
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan states the department 'continues to mature its regulatory performance framework' and 'is not considered a traditional regulator', and commits to progress a national labour hire licensing scheme — a genuinely new function needing design help. SCF restrictions on core policy work temper confidence.

recommended_actions.action: Approach the Employment and Workforce Group and the Integrity Unit with a tailored independent-assurance proposition for the mutual obligations compliance system
recommended_actions.rationale: This is named as 'critical work', is Robodebt-sensitive, and assurance falls outside the Strategic Commissioning Framework's core-work limits — making it both high-value and procurable.

recommended_actions.action: Prepare a capability statement on delivery assurance and evaluation for the employment services reform portfolio and place-based trials, targeting the relevant program branches
recommended_actions.rationale: Multiple named trials with an explicit evidence-for-reform purpose create discrete, time-bound delivery and evaluation needs that complement in-house teams.

recommended_actions.action: Engage the Chief Risk Officer / Audit and Risk Committee secretariat ahead of the 2025-26 governance framework review
recommended_actions.rationale: The review is committed in the plan; an early, well-scoped governance/committee-effectiveness offer can shape the work before it goes to market.

recommended_actions.action: Position digital build + Essential Eight cyber and data-analytics offerings with the Chief Information Officer, Chief Data Officer and CISO functions
recommended_actions.rationale: AI expansion, ICT modernisation and Essential Eight uplift are explicit commitments, and DEWR's heavy ICT-labour reliance shows existing appetite for external delivery capacity.

recommended_actions.action: Map the Strategic Commissioning Framework boundaries before pitching any policy/finance/service-delivery advisory
recommended_actions.rationale: DEWR is actively reducing outsourcing of core work; misjudging this wastes effort. Lead with assurance, evaluation, digital build and specialist surge, which are SCF-compatible.

engagement_targets: Employment and Workforce Group (employment services reform, mutual obligations compliance, place-based trials)
Integrity Unit and the independent integrity advisor function (Integrity Roadmap)
Chief Risk Officer and the Audit and Risk Committee / Risk Committee (governance review, enterprise risk)
Chief Information Officer / ICT delivery function (modernisation, Essential Eight, shared services)
Chief Data Officer and Data Governance Committee (Data Strategy, analytics, AI)
Skills and Training Group (tertiary roadmap, apprenticeship incentive reform, VET trials)
External Budgets, Strategy and Performance Branch / Strategy and Priorities Committee (performance and regulatory framework)
talking_points: Your plan makes delivery — not new reform — the 2025-26 priority; independent gateway-style assurance keeps named programs on time, on budget and demonstrably fair.

The mutual obligations compliance system is explicitly 'critical work' and Robodebt-adjacent — independent assurance of its design and controls protects the department and aligns with your Integrity Roadmap.

The Strategic Commissioning Framework means we'd lead with assurance, evaluation, digital build and specialist capacity — not core policy — so our work complements rather than competes with your in-house teams.
Your place-based trials are framed as evidence for broader reform; a rigorous evaluation turns that intent into defensible scaling decisions.
You've committed to expand AI and lift Essential Eight maturity — we can build and integrate, not just advise, and assure the security of the systems and data underneath.
The 2024 governance framework review is a natural moment for an independent committee-effectiveness and governance assessment.
caveats: This brief rests solely on the 2025-26 corporate plan; no external research (news, ANAO, Parliamentary) was supplied, so those sections are empty and demand signals are inferred from plan text only. The biggest commercial caveat is the Strategic Commissioning Framework: DEWR is deliberately reducing outsourcing of core work (policy, legal, accounting/finance, service delivery), which narrows credible opportunities to assurance, evaluation, digital/ICT build, cyber and specialist surge. The plan is light on dollar figures, procurement timing and named initiatives' budgets, so several opportunities are rated Medium/Low; confirm SCF boundaries and current panel arrangements before pursuing any advisory that touches core functions.
